original_abstract:
Isomerism, a fundamental concept in organic chemistry, has rarely been observed in covalent organic frameworks (COFs). In this study, we report the first successful controllable synthesis of topological isomers in a three-dimensional (3D) COF system. Utilizing a unique, partially flexible tetrahedral building block, 9,9´-bicarbazole (BC), we synthesized two distinct 3D isomers—designated JUC-620 with a diamondoid (dia) net and JUC-621 with a quartz (qtz) net—by modulating solvent polarity. Their structures were comprehensively elucidated using a combination of powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM), with JUC-621 representing the first example of a 3D COF possessing a qtz topology. 

Characterization revealed significant differences in the physical properties and secondary architectures of these isomers. JUC-621 features permanent mesopores (~23 Å) and a high BET surface area of 2060 m² g⁻¹, substantially outperforming its microporous isomer JUC-620 (~12 Å pores and 980 m² g⁻¹). Due to these enhanced structural features, JUC-621 demonstrated superior performance in environmental applications, efficiently removing large dye molecules and achieving an exceptional iodine adsorption capacity of 6.7 g g⁻¹—approximately 2.3 times that of JUC-620. This work establishes a strategy for constructing topological isomers in COFs and highlights the profound impact of network topology on the functional capabilities of porous materials.
humanized_abstract:
Isomerism is one of the basic concepts in organic chemistry. Unfortunately, isomerism is hardly observed in covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Here, we achieve the controllable synthesis of topological isomers for the first time in a three-dimensional (3D) COF system, using a partially flexible building block 9,9´-bicarbazole (BC) for the fabrication of two new 3D isomers, namely diamondoid dia (JUC-620) and quartz (qtz) JUC-621 networks, which are realized by adjusting the polarity of solvent medium. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization confirmed their crystal networks and further confirmed that JUC-621 is the first 3D COF with qtz topology.

In terms of structural characteristics, these isomers present distinct physical properties and secondary structures. While JUC-621 showed stable permanent mesopores of approximately 23 Å and displayed a higher BET surface area of 2060 m² g⁻¹ than that of its microporous isomer JUC-620 with mesopores of approximately 12 Å and a BET surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. As a result, JUC-621 exhibited more excellent performance in environmental science experiments such as removing larger dye molecules. In addition, the iodine adsorption capacity of JUC-621 reached 6.7 g g⁻¹ which is about 2.3 times higher than that of JUC-620. This work not only presents an approach for preparing topological isomers in COFs but also clarified the effects of the COF topology on the material properties.

document.output:
Isomerism is one of the basic concepts in organic chemistry. Unfortunately, isomerism is hardly observed in covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Here, we achieve the controllable synthesis of topological isomers for the first time in a three-dimensional (3D) COF system, using a partially flexible building block 9,9´-bicarbazole (BC) for the fabrication of two new 3D isomers, namely diamondoid dia (JUC-620) and quartz (qtz) JUC-621 networks, which are realized by adjusting the polarity of solvent medium. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization confirmed their crystal networks and further confirmed that JUC-621 is the first 3D COF with qtz topology.

In terms of structural characteristics, these isomers present distinct physical properties and secondary structures. While JUC-621 showed stable permanent mesopores of approximately 23 Å and displayed a higher BET surface area of 2060 m² g⁻¹ than that of its microporous isomer JUC-620 with mesopores of approximately 12 Å and a BET surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. As a result, JUC-621 exhibited more excellent performance in environmental science experiments such as removing larger dye molecules. In addition, the iodine adsorption capacity of JUC-621 reached 6.7 g g⁻¹ which is about 2.3 times higher than that of JUC-620. This work not only presents an approach for preparing topological isomers in COFs but also clarified the effects of the COF topology on the material properties.
document.input:
Isomerism, a fundamental concept in organic chemistry, has rarely been observed in covalent organic frameworks (COFs). In this study, we report the first successful controllable synthesis of topological isomers in a three-dimensional (3D) COF system. Utilizing a unique, partially flexible tetrahedral building block, 9,9´-bicarbazole (BC), we synthesized two distinct 3D isomers—designated JUC-620 with a diamondoid (dia) net and JUC-621 with a quartz (qtz) net—by modulating solvent polarity. Their structures were comprehensively elucidated using a combination of powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM), with JUC-621 representing the first example of a 3D COF possessing a qtz topology. 

Characterization revealed significant differences in the physical properties and secondary architectures of these isomers. JUC-621 features permanent mesopores (~23 Å) and a high BET surface area of 2060 m² g⁻¹, substantially outperforming its microporous isomer JUC-620 (~12 Å pores and 980 m² g⁻¹). Due to these enhanced structural features, JUC-621 demonstrated superior performance in environmental applications, efficiently removing large dye molecules and achieving an exceptional iodine adsorption capacity of 6.7 g g⁻¹—approximately 2.3 times that of JUC-620. This work establishes a strategy for constructing topological isomers in COFs and highlights the profound impact of network topology on the functional capabilities of porous materials.
